ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic mushroom dish class; no specific Mushroom Do Pyaza entry exists. FOODON_03311737 (mushroom dish) used as closest defensible anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers cooked mushrooms as a category. No specific Do Pyaza preparation code exists; A0CKP is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked mushroom dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept for Mushroom Do Pyaza or equivalent Indian mushroom curry found in publicly available SNOMED CT browser. Null assigned honestly.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Mushroom Do Pyaza is approximately 250 g, served as a main curry dish alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Mushroom curry, cooked — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition); supplemented with USDA FoodData Central mushroom and onion cooked component data for minor nutrients
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides values for mushroom curry (cooked, with oil and spices). Macros and key micronutrients are drawn from this source. Minor micronutrients (potassium, phosphorus, selenium, vitamin B-series) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked mushrooms (FDC ID 169251) and cooked onion (FDC ID 170000), weighted approximately 60% mushroom and 40% onion-spice base by mass. Sodium is estimated from typical Indian restaurant curry preparation using ICMR-NIN guidance on added salt.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed vegetable curry and cooked mushroom dishes — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate based on mushroom-onion curry category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Mushroom Do Pyaza exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 35 (low) based on the closest analogous entries: mixed vegetable curry dishes with onion and spice base typically report GI values of 30–40 in the Sydney database. Mushrooms have a very low GI (approximately 10–15 as a raw ingredient); the onion-spice base and cooking oil raise the effective dish GI modestly. A conservative estimate of 35 is used. GL per serving = (35 × 6.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 250 g serving) / 100 = 5.7 g. This is a low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends in restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 250 g restaurant serving of Mushroom Do Pyaza contributes approximately 800 mg sodium, which represents about 35% of the 2300 mg daily limit recommended for hypertensive individuals; patients should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~35) and low glycemic load (~5.7 per serving) due to mushroom and onion base with minimal starchy carbohydrate
type2_diabetes.guidance: Mushroom Do Pyaza has a low GI of approximately 35 and a low GL of approximately 5.7 per 250 g serving, making it a suitable vegetable curry choice for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without high-GI accompaniments such as white rice or naan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and presence of potassium and dietary fibre from mushrooms and onions
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Mushroom Do Pyaza is low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g) and contains no cholesterol, with potassium (~310 mg per 100 g) and dietary fibre (~1.8 g per 100 g) that support cardiovascular health; the primary concern is restaurant-level sodium, which should be moderated for patients with cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Mushroom Do Pyaza (also spelled Mushroom Dopiaza or Mushroom Do Piaza) is a classic North Indian vegetarian curry in which button mushrooms are sautéed in oil with onion paste and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, ginger, garlic, and garam masala, then finished with a generous garnish of sliced or diced seasoned onions. The name 'Do Pyaza' derives from the Urdu and Hindi words meaning 'two onions' or 'double onion', referring to the technique of adding onion at two stages of cooking — first as a paste in the base gravy and again as a textural garnish at the end. The dish has a rich, deeply savoury, mildly spiced flavour profile with earthy umami notes from the mushrooms, a slight sweetness from the caramelised onion, and a warm heat from the spice blend. The texture is semi-dry to semi-gravy, with tender mushroom pieces coated in a thick, aromatic sauce. Nutritionally, Mushroom Do Pyaza is a low-calorie, low-GI vegetable dish providing approximately 78 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (3.2 g), low saturated fat (0.6 g), and useful amounts of dietary fibre (1.8 g), potassium (310 mg), and B-vitamins including riboflavin and niacin from the mushrooms. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. The primary clinical concern is sodium from restaurant preparation, which can reach 800 mg per serving. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI (~35) and low GL (~5.7 per serving) make this a favourable curry choice. In Singapore, Mushroom Do Pyaza is served across multiple North Indian restaurants in the Boat Quay and South Bridge Road areas and is typically priced between SGD 15.90 and SGD 16.90 as a main dish, served with naan, roti, or rice.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Mushroom Do Pyaza?
Is Mushroom Do Pyaza good for diabetics?
Where to eat Mushroom Do Pyaza in Singapore?
meal_context: served with naan, roti, or basmati rice

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Mushroom Do Pyaza exists in ICMR-NIN or any other approved primary source. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN mushroom curry data (2017) supplemented with USDA FoodData Central cooked mushroom and onion component data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using comparable mixed vegetable curry entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) mushroom curry entry, supplemented with USDA FoodData Central cooked mushroom (FDC 169251) and cooked onion (FDC 170000) data for minor micronutrients. Sodium is estimated at 320 mg per 100 g based on typical North Indian restaurant curry preparation; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients; mushrooms and onions contain no vitamin B12. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as the dish uses vegetable oil and no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as all ingredients are plant-based. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Mushroom Do Pyaza was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. Serving size of 250 g is an estimate based on typical North Indian restaurant curry portion sizes. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 and FoodEx2 A0CKP are closest_parent matches only; no exact ontology code for Mushroom Do Pyaza exists in publicly available ontology browsers. Multilingual names: Chinese names (双葱蘑菇 / 雙蔥蘑菇) are descriptive translations meaning 'double-onion mushroom'; no established loanword exists in Chinese for this dish. Tamil and Hindi names are transliterations of the dish name. This record was generated from field survey data seed data (GD000026) and has not been validated against a live restaurant menu.
